pr.title: Add rate limiting middleware to API gateway
pr.author: dev-jane
pr.description: Implements token bucket rate limiting for all public API endpoints. Adds per-user and per-IP limits configurable via environment variables. Includes Redis-backed distributed counter support.

diff_summary.summary: New RateLimiter class implementing token bucket algorithm with Redis backend
Wire rate limiting middleware into request pipeline
Add RATE_LIMIT_* env var configuration with defaults
Redis-backed distributed counter for rate limiting across multiple gateway instances
Unit tests for token bucket logic and Redis backend
Integration tests using a real Redis instance via pytest fixture
Operator documentation for configuring and monitoring rate limits

panelist_reviews.summary: Rate limiting logic is sound but the Redis key schema leaks PII (raw user IDs in plaintext keys) and there is no fallback behavior when Redis is unavailable — the middleware currently raises an unhandled exception that would take down the gateway.

concerns.description: Redis keys are constructed as `rate:{user_id}:{endpoint}`. If user_id is an email address or internal UUID tied to PII, this violates data minimization principles. Keys should use a one-way hash.

RateLimiterRedis.increment() does not catch redis.exceptions.ConnectionError. A Redis outage will propagate a 500 to all API callers. The fallback should either allow traffic (fail-open) or use the in-memory bucket.

Token bucket refill calculation uses time.time() which is not monotonic. Clock skew on the host or NTP adjustments could cause negative token grants. Use time.monotonic() for elapsed time calculations.

panelist_reviews.praise: Good test coverage including integration tests. The config defaults are sensible and well-documented.

panelist_reviews.summary: The Redis Lua script for atomic increment is correct, but the middleware is applied globally before authentication, meaning unauthenticated requests consume the per-user quota anonymously under the IP bucket, which may be unintentional. Also missing: rate limit headers in the response.

concerns.description: Middleware is registered before the auth middleware in app.py. Authenticated and unauthenticated requests should likely have separate quota pools. Consider reordering or adding a flag to skip per-user limiting when no identity is resolved.

RFC 6585 and common practice require returning X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset headers. These are absent from the response, making it impossible for clients to implement backoff.
No metrics emitted when a request is throttled. A counter like `gateway.rate_limit.rejected{endpoint, reason}` would help with alerting.

panelist_reviews.praise: Lua atomic increment avoids race conditions cleanly. The token bucket parameters being per-endpoint via config is a nice touch.

panelist_reviews.summary: Documentation is clear and the feature is well-scoped. The security and reliability concerns raised by other panelists should be addressed before merge, but the overall approach is correct.

concerns.description: docs/rate-limiting.md does not document the 429 response body schema. Consumers need to know the shape of the error payload to display useful messages.

panelist_reviews.praise: Excellent linked issue references and a well-written PR description. The operator docs cover the most common tuning scenarios.

ceo_verdict.rationale: Two high-severity issues (PII in Redis keys, unhandled Redis failure) must be resolved before merge. The missing rate-limit response headers are a breaking omission for client compatibility. Changes are well-scoped and tests exist; a focused revision pass should be quick.
blocking_issues: Hash user identifiers before using them as Redis key components
Add fail-open (or in-memory fallback) when Redis is unreachable
Emit X-RateLimit-* response headers on every request
non_blocking_suggestions: Clarify middleware ordering relative to auth in docs
